questions.question: Define computer vision and explain how it differs from image processing and computer graphics. Give examples of applications in each field.
keyPoints: Computer vision extracts semantic understanding from images (2D to 3D understanding)
Image processing transforms images (image-to-image, e.g., filtering, enhancement)
Computer graphics creates images from 3D models (3D to 2D, inverse of CV)
CV example: autonomous driving object detection
IP example: noise removal, contrast enhancement
CG example: movie visual effects, video game rendering

questions.question: Describe the evolution of computer vision from the 1960s to the deep learning era. Mention key milestones.
keyPoints: 1963: Larry Roberts' PhD thesis on 3D from 2D images
1966: MIT Summer Vision Project revealed problem's complexity
1970s: Block world era, simple polyhedral object recognition
1982: David Marr's computational theory of vision (primal sketch, 2.5D sketch, 3D model)
1986: Canny edge detection
1999: SIFT features by David Lowe
2001: Viola-Jones real-time face detector
2012: AlexNet wins ImageNet, deep learning revolution begins

questions.question: List and explain the major challenges in computer vision with examples.
keyPoints: Illumination variation: same object looks different under different lighting
Viewpoint variation: objects appear different from different angles
Scale variation: objects at different distances appear at different sizes
Occlusion: parts of objects hidden by other objects
Background clutter: complex backgrounds interfere with recognition
Intra-class variation: objects of the same category look different (e.g., dog breeds)

questions.question: Explain the typical computer vision pipeline with a diagram. Describe each stage briefly.
keyPoints: Image Acquisition: capturing images via camera or sensor
Preprocessing: noise removal, normalization, enhancement
Feature Extraction: detecting edges, corners, descriptors
Feature Representation: encoding features as vectors or histograms
Learning/Recognition: classification, detection using ML/DL
Post-processing: refinement, non-maximum suppression
Decision/Action: final output or control signal
